section.title: Paul's Gospel from God
section.sectionRationale: Having pronounced a curse on any rival gospel, Paul now defends where his gospel came from. It was not devised by man nor taught to him, but received by revelation from Jesus Christ. He tells his own story, from zealous persecutor to called apostle, and shows that he owed his message to God, not to Jerusalem, so that the churches even glorified God because of him.
section.thesis: Paul's gospel is authoritative because it came by revelation from Jesus Christ, not from human tradition; the same God who set him apart before birth and turned the persecutor into a preacher is the guarantee that the message of grace is God's own and cannot be improved by human addition.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the claim that the gospel came by revelation, not from man; Paul's former life in Judaism and God's gracious call to reveal His Son in him; and Paul's limited early contact with Jerusalem, proving his independence, with the churches glorifying God.

units.label: Not devised by man
units.note: received by revelation from Jesus Christ

verses.text: For I want you to know, brothers, that the gospel I preached was not devised by man.
I did not receive it from any man, nor was I taught it; rather, I received it by {{apokalypsis|revelation}} from Jesus Christ.

units.label: From persecutor to called
units.note: God set him apart and called him by grace

verses.text: For you have heard of my former way of life in {{ioudaismos|Judaism}}, how severely I persecuted the church of God and tried to destroy it.
I was advancing in Judaism beyond many of my contemporaries and was extremely zealous for the traditions of my fathers.
But when God, who set me apart from my mother's womb and called me by His {{charis|grace}}, was pleased
to reveal His Son in me so that I might preach Him among the Gentiles, I did not rush to consult with flesh and blood,
nor did I go up to Jerusalem to the apostles who came before me, but I went into Arabia and later returned to Damascus.

units.label: Little contact with Jerusalem
units.note: unknown by face, they glorified God

verses.text: Only after three years did I go up to Jerusalem to confer with Cephas, and I stayed with him fifteen days.
But I saw none of the other apostles except James, the Lord's brother.
I assure you before God that what I am writing to you is no lie.
Later I went to the regions of Syria and Cilicia.
I was personally unknown, however, to the churches of Judea that are in Christ.
They only heard the account: “The man who formerly persecuted us is now preaching the faith he once tried to destroy.”
And they glorified God because of me.

apokalypsis.gloss: A revelation, an unveiling, a disclosure of what was hidden. Paul insists his gospel came by apokalypsis from Jesus Christ, not by human instruction. The word points back to the Damascus road, where the risen Christ made Himself known and commissioned Paul directly, so the message carries divine, not merely human, authority.

ioudaismos.gloss: Judaism, the whole way of life ordered by the law and the traditions of the fathers. Paul had excelled in it and was violently zealous for it, which makes his conversion all the more striking. Only God's intervention could turn so committed a defender of the traditions into a preacher of grace to the Gentiles.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's unearned favor. Paul says God called him by His grace, verse 15, while he was still a persecutor of the church. Grace, not merit, explains both his salvation and his commission; the man who tried to destroy the church became its apostle purely by God's free favor.

basic.context: Paul keeps defending the gospel by defending where it came from. His opponents may have said his message was second hand, learned from the Jerusalem apostles and then softened. Paul answers with his own story: he received the gospel directly from the risen Christ. He was a fierce persecutor, God called him by grace, and only later did he have brief contact with Jerusalem, so his gospel is God's, not man's.
basic.hermeneutics: This is autobiography with a purpose. Every detail, the persecuting past, the direct call, the years away, the short visits, serves one point: Paul's gospel was not handed down by human teachers and cannot be edited by them. Read it as Paul's evidence that grace is God's own message.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul says he received the gospel by apokalypsis, revelation, an unveiling by the risen Christ. He describes his former life in ioudaismos, Judaism, where he was violently zealous. And he traces everything to charis, grace, for God called him while he was still a persecutor.
basic.typology: God setting Paul apart from his mother's womb echoes the calling of the prophets, Jeremiah and the servant of Isaiah, chosen before birth for God's purposes. Paul's turnaround pictures the gospel he preaches: the enemy of God is reconciled by grace and made a herald of the very faith he opposed.
basic.christ: The center of the passage is that God was pleased to reveal His Son in Paul. Christ is the content of the gospel and the one who commissions Paul directly. The risen Jesus is the source of Paul's message, which is why no human authority can override it.
basic.god: God set Paul apart before birth, called him by grace, and was pleased to reveal His Son in him. God takes the initiative at every step, choosing, calling, and revealing. The result is that God gets the glory: the Judean churches glorified God because of what He had done in Paul.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 9 tells of Paul's conversion on the Damascus road. Jeremiah 1 speaks of being set apart before birth. Philippians 3 lists Paul's credentials in Judaism, now counted as loss for Christ.
basic.oneStory: God delights to make enemies into worshipers. Paul the persecutor becomes Paul the apostle, a living sample of the grace he preaches. The story runs on the same grace that will save the Galatians, the grace the false teachers are in danger of trading away.

context.literary: This is the start of Paul's autobiographical defense, which runs into chapter 2. It is structured to prove independence: the emphatic denials in verses 11 and 12, the story of his call, and then a careful timeline of his few and brief contacts with Jerusalem, sealed by an oath in verse 20.
context.historical: Paul was a Pharisee trained in the strictest traditions, a leading persecutor of the early church. Damascus, Arabia, Syria, and Cilicia trace his early movements after conversion. His point is that for years he was largely unknown to the Judean churches, so his gospel could not have been derived from them.
context.canonical: The passage is a primary source for Paul's conversion and calling, complementing the accounts in Acts. It anchors the New Testament conviction that the apostolic gospel is revealed by God, a foundation for the authority of Paul's letters.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To prove that Paul's gospel came by direct revelation from Jesus Christ, not from human teachers, so that its authority is God's own and it cannot be corrected or supplemented by the agitators or even by Jerusalem.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes Paul's unique conversion and calling; it is not a template for how everyone must be converted. The abiding truths are that the gospel is God's revelation and that grace can transform even a violent opponent into a servant of Christ.

debates.question: Does Paul's independence from Jerusalem undermine the unity of the apostles?

views.label: Independent yet in agreement
views.case: Paul received his gospel directly from Christ and later found the Jerusalem leaders preached the same gospel, chapter 2; independence of source does not mean a different message.

views.label: A rivalry between Paul and Jerusalem
views.case: Some read tension between Paul and the pillars, but Paul's own account stresses that they added nothing to him and extended fellowship, showing agreement, not rivalry.

debates.bearing: Paul's aim is not to pit himself against the other apostles but to show his gospel is from God, so it stands even if some in Jerusalem's orbit were being misused by the agitators.

study.originalLanguages: The key word is apokalypsis, revelation, the direct unveiling of Christ that gave Paul his gospel apart from any human teacher. His old life was lived in ioudaismos, Judaism, with a zeal so fierce it drove him to persecute the church. The turn came entirely by charis, grace, for God called him while he was still an enemy and was pleased to reveal His Son in him. The transformation of the persecutor into the preacher is itself an argument: only God could have done it, so the message is God's.

typology.fulfillment: Echoes the prophetic call of Jeremiah 1:5 and the servant of Isaiah 49:1, chosen before birth, now fulfilled in Paul's commission to the nations.
Pictures the gospel itself, the enemy of God reconciled by grace and sent to proclaim the faith he once tried to destroy.

study.christ: Christ stands at the heart of the passage as the one who reveals Himself to Paul. God was pleased to reveal His Son in Paul so that he might preach Him among the Gentiles. The risen Jesus is both the message and the messenger's authority. Because the gospel comes from Christ directly, it cannot be downgraded to one human opinion among many, and it cannot be improved by adding the works of the law.
study.god: God is the great initiator here. He set Paul apart before birth, called him by grace while he was a persecutor, and was pleased to reveal His Son in him. Salvation and calling alike flow from God's free choice, not Paul's merit, which is the very grace the letter defends. The passage ends with God rightly getting the glory, for the churches glorified God because of the change in Paul.

crossReferences.note: The Damascus road conversion, the revelation of the risen Christ that lies behind verse 12.
God setting a servant apart from the womb, echoed in verse 15.
Paul's standing in Judaism, now counted as loss compared to knowing Christ.
Paul the former blasphemer and persecutor shown mercy as a pattern of grace.

study.oneStory: The Bible is full of God making enemies into friends and rebels into heralds. Paul is a vivid instance: the man who tried to destroy the church becomes its apostle, and the churches glorify God for it. The grace that seized Paul is the same grace that saves the Galatians, and Paul tells his story to keep them from trading that grace for a gospel of human tradition.

sermon.title: No Human Invention (Galatians 1:11 to 24)
sermon.why: Begg expounds Paul's insistence that the gospel was not a human invention but a revelation from Jesus Christ, tracing Paul's conversion and independence to show that the message of grace carries God's own authority.

quiz.q: Where does Paul say his gospel came from?

options.text: By revelation from Jesus Christ, not from any man.
options.note: Verse 12 grounds the gospel in direct revelation from Christ.

options.text: From careful study of the traditions of his fathers.
options.note: Those traditions marked his old life; the gospel came by revelation, not tradition.

options.text: From the apostles who trained him in Jerusalem.
options.note: Paul stresses he did not receive it from the Jerusalem apostles.

options.text: From his own clever reasoning.
options.note: He denies it was devised by man at all.

quiz.q: What was Paul's life like before God called him?

options.text: He violently persecuted the church and was zealous for the traditions.
options.note: Verses 13 and 14 describe his fierce opposition to the church.

options.text: He was already a quiet follower of Jesus.
options.note: The opposite; he tried to destroy the church.

options.text: He was indifferent to religion altogether.
options.note: He was extremely zealous, not indifferent.

options.text: He was a Gentile with no interest in the law.
options.note: He advanced in Judaism beyond his contemporaries.

quiz.q: Why does Paul stress his limited early contact with Jerusalem?

options.text: To show his gospel came from God directly, not second hand from other men.
options.note: The timeline proves the independence of his message.

options.text: To prove he disagreed with all the other apostles.
options.note: He shows independence of source, not a different gospel.

options.text: To boast that he never needed other believers.
options.note: The point is the origin of his gospel, not self sufficiency.

options.text: Because he was ashamed of the Jerusalem church.
options.note: He honors the church; the churches glorified God because of him.

options.text: God set him apart and called him by grace.
options.note: Verse 15 credits grace, not merit, for his calling.

options.text: His own decision to become more religious.
options.note: He was already intensely religious; the change came from God's grace.

options.text: Pressure from the Jerusalem apostles.
options.note: The call came from God directly, not from apostolic pressure.

options.text: A gradual change of mind over many years.
options.note: God's gracious call and revelation of His Son were the cause.

quiz.q: How did the Judean churches respond to news about Paul?

options.text: They glorified God because the persecutor was now preaching the faith.
options.note: Verse 24 shows God got the glory for the change in Paul.

options.text: They refused to believe he had really changed.
options.note: They heard the account and glorified God, rather than rejecting it.

options.text: They resented that he was preaching without their permission.
options.note: The text records worship, not resentment.

options.text: They ignored the news entirely.
options.note: They actively glorified God because of him.

questions: Paul's gospel came by revelation, not human invention. How does it change your confidence to know the gospel is God's message and not a human theory?
Paul was a zealous, religious man who was still opposed to God. Where might sincere religious effort actually be keeping someone from grace?
God called Paul while he was still a persecutor. Does anyone seem beyond the reach of grace to you, and how does Paul's story challenge that?
The churches glorified God because of the change in Paul. When people look at your life, what do they end up giving God credit for?
Paul refused to let anyone edit the gospel he received. What pressures tempt you to soften or add to the message of grace today?

translationNotes.note: Revelation translates apokalypsis, an unveiling; Paul points to the risen Christ making Himself known on the Damascus road as the source of his gospel.
Reveal His Son in me can also be rendered reveal His Son to me and through me; either way the emphasis is on God's direct disclosure of Christ.
Cephas is the Aramaic name for Peter, as the BSB footnote indicates; the two names refer to the same apostle.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Galatians 1:11-24. No em dashes in this passage required substitution. Sermon verified this session from the Truth For Life series A Study in Galatians, Volume 1 by Alistair Begg.
